# Why You Get Sciatica - The REAL Reason, & How To Stop It.

Sep 5

Written By [Louis Ellery](https://www.functionalpatternsbrisbane.com/blog-page?author=58babd56d482e9728b83cf44)

Sciatica pain can be an incredibly debilitating condition, and many individuals suffering from it often turn to stretching and mobility exercises for relief. While these exercises may offer temporary relief, they often fail to address the root cause of the problem. In our exploration of sciatica pain, we've discovered that it is frequently linked to a posterior pelvic tilt—a structural issue that can lead to a compressed spine and tight muscles, ultimately triggering sciatica pain.

The positioning of your body's structure plays a pivotal role in whether or not you experience pain. No matter how many stretching and mobility exercises you do, if you don't reprogram your muscles to correct your pelvic position, these temporary solutions will only provide limited relief. That's why we advocate for a comprehensive systems approach to address pain in the body. By understanding what's happening on a systemic level, we can unveil the root causes of symptoms like joint pain, muscle aches, and discomfort.

In this blog, we'll delve deeper into the relationship between sciatica pain and pelvic positioning. We'll explain why correcting your pelvic alignment and enhancing glute and core function through rotation are crucial steps in permanently alleviating sciatica pain. Let's explore how addressing the root cause can provide long-lasting relief.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5d38d40f73a6ec0001796a6d/a15d36f7-0255-4c51-ab60-59ca12452295/Screenshot+2023-09-05+at+8.55.43+am.jpg)

## The Role of Pelvic Positioning in Sciatica Pain:

Sciatica pain often arises from structural imbalances, and one of the most common culprits is a posterior pelvic tilt. In this position, the pelvis tilts backward, causing issues such as a compressed spine and tight muscles. These problems can directly trigger sciatica pain.

Understanding the Impact:

- **Compressed Spine:** A posterior pelvic tilt can exert undue pressure on the spine, leading to nerve compression—the hallmark of sciatica pain.

- **Muscle Tightness:** This tilt can result in tightness in the muscles surrounding the pelvis, exacerbating pain and discomfort.


![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5d38d40f73a6ec0001796a6d/4e94d495-3970-410b-91f4-b3501ec526c2/FP+Web+Graphics+%281%29.png)

## The Limits of Stretching and Mobility Exercises:

Stretching and mobility exercises are commonly employed to alleviate sciatica pain. While they can offer temporary relief by releasing tension and increasing flexibility, they often fall short of addressing the root problem—pelvic positioning.

**Temporary Relief:** These exercises may provide temporary comfort by relieving muscle tension and improving mobility. Inadequate Solutions: Without correcting the underlying structural issue, these exercises can only go so far in providing long-lasting relief.

## A Systems Approach to Sciatica Pain:

To truly conquer sciatica pain, we must adopt a systems approach that looks beyond surface symptoms and delves into the root causes. By examining how your body functions as a whole, we can pinpoint systemic imbalances that contribute to pain.

**Systemic Understanding:** This approach involves understanding how various factors, including pelvic positioning and muscle function, interplay to create or alleviate pain. Holistic Solutions: By addressing the systemic issues, we can provide holistic solutions that target the root cause of sciatica pain.

## Correcting Pelvic Positioning and Enhancing Function:

The key to permanently resolving sciatica pain lies in correcting your pelvic alignment and enhancing the function of essential muscles, particularly the glutes and core, through rotational exercises.

**Pelvic Alignment:** Restoring a neutral pelvic position can relieve the pressure on the spine and alleviate nerve compression, mitigating sciatica pain. Glute and Core Function: Rotation exercises engage and strengthen the glutes and core, promoting balanced muscle function and helping to maintain proper pelvic alignment.

In the battle against sciatica pain, stretching and mobility exercises are valuable tools for temporary relief, but they fall short of addressing the root cause. By recognising the pivotal role of pelvic positioning and adopting a systems approach to pain, we can uncover the systemic imbalances that contribute to sciatica symptoms.

To achieve lasting relief from sciatica pain, focus on correcting your pelvic alignment and enhancing glute and core function through rotation exercises. By addressing the structural issues at the root of your pain, you can pave the way for a life free from the constraints of sciatica discomfort.
